beats.prompt: Warm up: match each term to its definition.

pairs.left: a cell no one wants to leave

feedback.correct: Good recall. Today: when no dominant strategy exists, we find the cell nobody wants to leave — the Nash equilibrium.
hints: A dominant strategy beats every opponent response — it is best no matter what the opponent does.
In the Prisoner's Dilemma both players confess, landing at (Defect, Defect) — the only stable outcome.
A Nash equilibrium is a cell where neither player gains by switching alone — nobody wants to leave.

beats.prompt: Two hunters can team up to catch a stag (big reward) or hunt alone for a hare (small, safe reward). Stag requires both; hare succeeds alone. Payoffs (row player, column player): (Stag, Stag) = (3, 3); (Stag, Hare) = (0, 1); (Hare, Stag) = (1, 0); (Hare, Hare) = (1, 1). How many stable, self-enforcing outcomes does this game have?

0.note: There are stable outcomes. At (Stag, Stag) neither hunter wants to switch to Hare unilaterally (3 > 1). At (Hare, Hare) neither wants to switch to Stag alone (0 < 1). Two self-enforcing cells exist.

1.note: Not just one. (Stag, Stag) is self-enforcing: if your partner hunts Stag, your best response is also Stag (3 > 1). But (Hare, Hare) is also self-enforcing: if your partner hunts Hare, switching to Stag alone earns 0 — worse than 1. Both are Nash equilibria.

2.note: Yes — (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) are both stable. In each, neither hunter benefits from deviating alone. This game has two pure Nash equilibria.

interaction.title: Best response and Nash equilibrium
interaction.body: Your best response is the strategy that maximizes your payoff given what your opponent is doing — the best row for a given column, or the best column for a given row. A Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile where BOTH players are simultaneously best-responding: neither player can improve by switching alone. Equivalently, it is a cell that is a mutual best response — a cell no one wants to leave.

feedback.correct: Hold onto that: Nash equilibrium = mutual best response = nobody wants to deviate alone.
hints: Best response: given the opponent's strategy, the highest-payoff reply.
Nash: BOTH players are best-responding at once — mutual, not one-sided.
Read and tap Continue.

beats.prompt: Stag Hunt: tap each row and column header to mark best responses. A cell doubly marked — by both players — is a Nash equilibrium.

feedback.correct: Best responses: if the other hunts Stag, your best response is Stag (3 > 1); if the other hunts Hare, your best response is Hare (1 > 0). The doubly-marked cells — (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) — are the two Nash equilibria.
hints: Tap the Stag column: Hunter A gets 3 (Stag) vs 1 (Hare) — best response is Stag.
Tap the Hare column: Hunter A gets 0 (Stag) vs 1 (Hare) — best response is Hare.
Doubly-marked cells (best response for both players) are Nash equilibria: (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare).

hero.structuralReadout: Best response to Stag is Stag (3>1); to Hare is Hare (1>0) — two doubly-marked cells, two Nash equilibria.

beats.prompt: Coordination game: two colleagues want to meet at one of two locations. Tap the single pure Nash equilibrium — the cell where neither person gains from showing up elsewhere.

feedback.correct: (Meet A, Meet A) is the unique Nash equilibrium. At (Meet A, Meet A) both earn 3; switching to Meet B unilaterally drops your payoff to 2 or 1. (Meet B, Meet B) is not stable: either player could earn 2 by switching to A instead of 0.
hints: At (Meet A, Meet A): Alice earns 3. If she switches to Meet B, she earns 2 — no incentive.
At (Meet A, Meet A): Bob earns 3. Switching to Meet B gives him 2 — no incentive either.
At (Meet B, Meet B): Alice earns 0. Switching to Meet A gives her 2 — she would deviate. Not stable.

beats.prompt: Back to Stag Hunt. Tap ALL pure Nash equilibrium cells — every cell that nobody would leave unilaterally.

feedback.correct: Both (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) are pure Nash equilibria. At (Stag, Stag) neither hunter gains by switching to Hare (1 < 3). At (Hare, Hare) neither gains by switching to Stag (0 < 1). Two equilibria, no dominant strategy.
hints: Test (Stag, Stag): if Hunter A switches to Hare, payoff drops from 3 to 1. No incentive to deviate — stable.
Test (Hare, Hare): if Hunter A switches to Stag, payoff drops from 1 to 0. No incentive to deviate — also stable.
Test (Stag, Hare): Hunter B earns 1. If B switches to Stag, payoff rises to 3 — B would deviate. Not stable. Tap (0,0) and (1,1).

hero.structuralReadout: Two cells survive the deviation test: (Stag, Stag) and (Hare, Hare) — both are Nash equilibria.

beats.prompt: Stag Hunt has two equilibria. Which one will rational players reach? Tap each lens to see three ways to analyze the choice.

lenses.label: Payoff-dominant
lenses.body: (Stag, Stag) = (3, 3) gives both players higher payoffs than (Hare, Hare) = (1, 1). If both hunters trust each other to coordinate on the better outcome, they both earn more. Payoff dominance selects (Stag, Stag).

lenses.label: Risk-dominant
lenses.body: Hunting Hare guarantees at least 1 regardless of what the other player does. Hunting Stag alone earns 0 — a painful miss. A risk-averse hunter who is uncertain about the other's choice prefers the safe option. Risk dominance selects (Hare, Hare).

lenses.label: Equilibrium selection
lenses.body: Neither criterion dominates on all dimensions: (Stag, Stag) is payoff-dominant but risky; (Hare, Hare) is safe but inefficient. Selecting between them requires a focal point — pre-game communication, social norms, reputation, or repeated interaction. Theory alone cannot predict which equilibrium emerges.

feedback.correct: (Stag, Stag) is payoff-dominant — both players prefer it when they trust each other to coordinate. (Hare, Hare) is risk-dominant — safer when you are uncertain. Equilibrium selection depends on context, culture, or coordination devices outside the stage-game payoffs.
hints: Payoff-dominant: the equilibrium that gives the highest payoff to everyone if they coordinate on it.
Risk-dominant: the equilibrium that is safest to play when you are uncertain about the other player's choice.
Focal point (Schelling point): a salient feature — communication, convention, or prior experience — that makes one equilibrium the obvious choice.

beats.interviewNote: Interviewers often ask 'how many Nash equilibria does this game have?' Solve it by the best-response method: mark each player's best-response arrows, then count the doubly-marked cells. If no cell is doubly marked, there are no pure NE and players must mix (L3). The three canonical counts are 1 (Prisoner's Dilemma), 2 (Stag Hunt, coordination games), and 0 (Matching Pennies).
beats.prompt: Match each game to its number of pure Nash equilibria.

feedback.correct: Prisoner's Dilemma: one NE — (Defect, Defect) — because Defect is strictly dominant for both players. Stag Hunt: two NE, no dominant strategy, two stable coordinations. Matching Pennies: zero pure NE — each player always wants to switch to surprise the other, so no pure cell is stable (requires a mixed equilibrium, L3).
hints: PD: Defect strictly dominates Cooperate for both → the unique NE is (Defect, Defect).
Stag Hunt: no dominant strategy; both diagonal cells are mutually best responses.
Matching Pennies: whatever pure strategy one player picks, the other wants to switch — no doubly-marked cell exists.

beats.prompt: Lock in the method with a new game.

interaction.scenario: Two drivers approach each other on a narrow road. Each can Swerve or go Straight. Payoffs (row player, column player): \((\text{Swerve},\text{Swerve})=(4,4)\), \((\text{Swerve},\text{Straight})=(2,5)\), \((\text{Straight},\text{Swerve})=(5,2)\), \((\text{Straight},\text{Straight})=(1,1)\). Apply the best-response method.

fields.label: Payoff to the driver who goes Straight while the other Swerves

feedback.correct: Best responses: if the other Swerves, going Straight pays \(5 > 4\) — so best response to Swerve is Straight; if the other goes Straight, Swerving pays \(2 > 1\) — so best response to Straight is Swerve. The two doubly-marked cells are \((\text{Swerve},\text{Straight})\) and \((\text{Straight},\text{Swerve})\) — the driver who goes Straight while the other Swerves earns \(5\). \[ \text{NE}_1 = (\text{Swerve},\text{Straight}),\quad \text{NE}_2 = (\text{Straight},\text{Swerve}). \]
hints: If the other player Swerves: compare your payoffs — Swerve earns 4, Straight earns 5. Best response is Straight.
If the other player goes Straight: compare — Swerve earns 2, Straight earns 1. Best response is Swerve.
Mark the arrows: (Swerve, Straight) and (Straight, Swerve) are both doubly marked — two pure Nash equilibria.

feedback.correct: A Nash equilibrium is a mutual best response — a cell where neither player gains by switching alone. Find all pure NE by the best-response method: mark each player's best responses and count the doubly-marked cells. Some games have 1 (Prisoner's Dilemma), 2 (Stag Hunt, Chicken), or 0 (Matching Pennies) pure NE. When none exist in pure strategies, players must mix — that is L3.
hints: Nash: each player is simultaneously best-responding to the other's strategy.
Best-response method: mark arrows for each player, count mutual intersections.
Bridge: L3 — when no pure NE exists, look for a mixed-strategy equilibrium.
